claim_text: In the currently coded Ohio matched triads, safety rows more often show formal impasse or interest-arbitration backstop language, while matched non-safety rows are better evidenced through classification, wage-grade, or grievance-administration channels.
current_scope: Current GABRIEL/codify evidence for Ohio matched cities only; Columbus, Cleveland, Cincinnati, and Toledo; verified-present rows only for primary support.
weakest_evidence_gap: Cincinnati fire and police-supervisor windows have zero verified-present rows; Cleveland fire has a previously flagged/unverified interest row excluded from support; codify windows are not full-document searches.
specific_sources_needed: Full-document or broader-window codify for Ohio non-safety rows; Ohio SERB impasse/arbitration materials for the same cities and cycles; repeat cycles in Columbus/Cleveland/Cincinnati/Toledo.
suggested_city_types: mid/large cities with police, fire, and general municipal non-safety CBAs
what_would_change_claim: Additional verified-present non-safety interest/impasse rows in Ohio matched cities, or failure to reproduce safety interest/impasse evidence in full-document coding, would weaken this claim.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: candidate (uningested) matched-triad-shaped sources now identified for Philadelphia PA, Newark NJ, Jersey City NJ (dated), and Trenton NJ. See docs/analysis/pa_nj_state_city_claim_notes_2026-07-12.md and state_city_claim_map_2026-07-12.csv.

claim_text: Texas evidence is consistent with institutionally uneven safety wage-setting, but the current Texas comparison is not a clean statewide safety-versus-non-safety design outside Houston.
current_scope: Current Texas codified contracts and report source inventory; Houston, Austin, and San Antonio only.
weakest_evidence_gap: Texas non-safety support outside Houston is thin; Austin EMS is not a clean non-safety comparator; San Antonio lacks a non-safety row; source-production effects may drive the pattern.
specific_sources_needed: More Texas non-safety sources if publicly available, especially San Antonio/Austin clerical, public works, or general municipal units; repeat Houston cycles.
suggested_city_types: states/cities with specialized safety channels and uncertain non-safety pathways
what_would_change_claim: Public, in-window San Antonio or Austin general municipal non-safety CBAs with comparable codified evidence would change the Texas comparison from structurally uneven to potentially triadic.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language.

claim_text: Massachusetts is the current corpus’s densest cross-occupation comparison base, supporting bounded safety/non-safety mechanism comparison in Franklin and Seekonk while still falling short of a uniform state grid.
current_scope: Current Massachusetts codified subset; primarily Franklin and Seekonk, with Boston, Georgetown, Somerville, and Wayland as partial additional context.
weakest_evidence_gap: Massachusetts remains partly uncodified; city coverage is uneven; current evidence is mechanism presence rather than wage outcomes.
specific_sources_needed: Codify the remaining Massachusetts rows; add repeat cycles in Franklin/Seekonk; add matched non-safety rows for Somerville/Newton safety units where available.
suggested_city_types: small/mid cities with multiple occupation CBAs posted together
what_would_change_claim: Codifying the remaining Massachusetts contracts, or finding that uncodified rows materially reverse the current Franklin/Seekonk pattern, would change this claim.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language.

claim_text: The strongest current safety mechanism pattern is not generic occupational hardship alone, but occupational pressure paired with channels that convert pressure into compensation language: overtime, callback, premium pay, minimum staffing, and formal impasse backstops.
current_scope: Current verified-present evidence across MA/TX/OH safety rows, with non-safety rows used as explicit counterpoints.
weakest_evidence_gap: No wage effect sizes; no national sample; codify detects presence not importance; pressure-to-compensation logic is inferred from co-occurrence and clause function.
specific_sources_needed: Repeat cycles for current cities; additional matched triads in states with public CBAs; full-document coding of minimum-staffing and premium-pay sections.
suggested_city_types: matched triads plus safety award/factfinding cities
what_would_change_claim: If broader evidence showed similar hazard/minimum-staffing/premium/impasse bundles in non-safety matched units, or safety rows lacked these channels in repeat cycles, this claim would weaken.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Philadelphia's city-hosted Act 111 interest-arbitration award (fire) is a strong uningested candidate for testing this claim in a second state.

claim_text: In the currently coded non-safety evidence, the clearest wage-setting channel is classification, grade, step, or administrative adjustment language rather than safety-specific hazard or minimum-staffing language.
current_scope: Current verified-present non-safety rows across Massachusetts and Ohio; Texas Houston non-safety treated as a counterpoint.
weakest_evidence_gap: The attribute may be under-detected because classification schedules can appear in appendices outside curated windows; the claim does not imply non-safety wages are weak or unpressured.
specific_sources_needed: Broader non-safety windows with wage appendices; more Texas non-safety units; repeat cycles in Franklin/Seekonk/Wayland/Cleveland.
suggested_city_types: cities with public works, clerical, library, teacher, sanitation CBAs
what_would_change_claim: Broader non-safety coding showing frequent hazard/minimum-staffing/impasse bundles, or sparse classification evidence in full-document windows, would revise the claim.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Reading PA (library CBA, direct PDF) and Trenton NJ (AFSCME 2281, direct in-window PDF) are the two strongest uningested non-safety candidates found across both scan rounds.

claim_text: The current evidence supports a report-ready methodological distinction between interest/formal impasse arbitration and grievance or contract-interpretation arbitration; the San Antonio police contract is the clearest within-document test.
current_scope: Current codify evidence and audits, especially San Antonio police, Toledo police, Houston fire, and Somerville police/JLMC award.
weakest_evidence_gap: This is a coding/method claim, not a substantive wage-gap claim; some windows may omit one arbitration form even when present elsewhere in a full document.
specific_sources_needed: Additional impasse/arbitration awards or factfinding documents in Ohio and Massachusetts; reviewer audit of all interest-arbitration positives.
suggested_city_types: cities with both grievance arbitration clauses and formal impasse sources
what_would_change_claim: If full-document review showed repeated conflation by codify, or if many interest-arbitration positives were actually grievance-only clauses, the distinction would need revision.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: Philadelphia's and Trenton/Newark's PERC-documented interest-arbitration histories are plausible candidates for a second/third state test of the arbitration distinction, pending ingestion.

claim_text: Peer/comparator wage evidence should currently be stated only as a narrow and underdeveloped claim: codify verifies one Somerville comparator-style row, while San Antonio shows a documented false negative.
current_scope: Current evidence layer plus expanded Texas/Ohio audit; not a general absence claim.
weakest_evidence_gap: Only one verified-present row; false negatives known; likely source-type mismatch because comparator reasoning may appear in arbitration/factfinding documents rather than base CBAs.
specific_sources_needed: Interest-arbitration awards, factfinding reports, wage studies, and broader windows around appendices/attachments; codebook refinement/re-run for comparator language.
suggested_city_types: states with public awards, factfinding, wage panels, or impasse decisions
what_would_change_claim: Additional verified comparator rows across multiple cities or a refined codebook re-run that captures San Antonio comparator language would move this from gap claim to substantive mechanism claim.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language. | PA/NJ follow-up 2026-07-12: no comparator-specific award/factfinding document was located this round; NJ's PERC interest-arbitration-awards index (IAAwards.nsf) remains the most promising unexploited lead for this urgent-priority claim.

claim_text: A substantive Texas non-safety comparison outside Houston is not report-ready: the current evidence has one genuine Houston non-safety source, one Austin safety-adjacent EMS source, and no San Antonio non-safety comparator.
current_scope: Texas source-inventory and current codified evidence only.
weakest_evidence_gap: No in-window San Antonio non-safety row; Austin EMS is safety-adjacent; Houston is a single-city base; no new source collection in this run.
specific_sources_needed: Targeted public-source verification for Texas non-safety units if available; otherwise document institutional non-availability as a design limitation.
suggested_city_types: cities where safety sources are public but non-safety pathways may be thin
what_would_change_claim: Public San Antonio or Austin general municipal CBAs, or credible evidence that such units are unavailable for institutional reasons, would change the status.
notes: Source expansion should test this claim before stronger report language.
